problem_statement: AI code generation models frequently produce insecure code that may enter the software supply chain without adequate security review, and current practices for evaluating and overseeing the security of AI-generated code are inconsistent and inadequate.
magnitude_evidence: 92% of surveyed U.S.-based developers report using AI coding tools (GitHub June 2023 survey); another Nov 2023 survey reported 96% of developers using AI coding tools; authors' own evaluation found ~48% of code snippets from five LLMs contained bugs potentially exploitable
insufficiency_evidence: burden of verifying AI-generated code is secure falls mainly on users whose willingness to expend costs is not constant; evaluation benchmarks focus on functionality not security; 76% of surveyed workers believe AI code is more secure than human code (automation bias); Perry et al. found users with AI assistants wrote significantly less secure code while believing it was secure
solution_mechanisms: evaluate all code regardless of authorship via secure SDLC
distribute responsibility across stakeholders (AI developers, orgs, policymakers)
remove vulnerable code from training datasets
assess models on security benchmarks not just functionality
built-in output security checks in tools
formal-verification / model checking (ESBMC) as vulnerability proxy
existing risk management frameworks (NIST C-SCRM, CSF)
